patterns.name: Heat vs Temperature Mix-Up
patterns.what: Tends to confuse gaining heat with a rise in temperature, forgetting that during melting or boiling, a substance gains heat but its temperature stays exactly the same.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Larisalami included the flat parts of the graph where the ice is melting, even though the temperature isn't increasing during those times.
He picked only the sloped lines, forgetting that the ice and water are still gaining heat even when the temperature line is flat.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see a graph with flat lines, remember:
- **Flat lines** mean the substance is changing state (like melting or boiling). It is still **gaining heat**, even though the temperature isn't going up!
- **Sloped lines** mean the temperature is actually changing.

patterns.name: Conductor vs Insulator Mix-Up
patterns.what: Occasionally mixes up which materials are good at trapping heat (insulators like plastic) and which are good at letting heat pass through quickly (conductors like metal).

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Larisalami picked a material that conducts heat well for a vacuum flask, but a flask needs a poor conductor to keep the water warm.
He chose a plastic container for the fastest cooling, but plastic traps heat; a metal container would let the heat escape much faster.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When a question asks about keeping things warm or cooling things down quickly, check the material:
- **Metals** are good conductors. They let heat travel fast, so they cool down hot water quickly.
- **Plastics and woods** are poor conductors (insulators). They trap heat, keeping things warm for a longer time.

patterns.name: Volume and Cooling Mix-Up
patterns.what: Sometimes forgets that a smaller amount of water will lose heat and cool down much faster than a larger amount of water.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Larisalami picked a graph showing the larger volume of water cooling faster, which is the opposite of what happens.
When trying this similar question again, he still mixed up how the different amounts of water affect the speed of cooling.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When comparing different amounts of hot water:
- **Less water** cools down faster because it has less heat energy to lose.
- **More water** takes a longer time to cool down. Always check the **volume (ml)** before picking the graph!

patterns.name: Path of Light Mix-Up
patterns.what: Occasionally traces the path of light backwards, forgetting that light must bounce off the object and travel into our eyes for us to see it.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Larisalami chose the option where light travels from the eye to the object, instead of from the object to the eye.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When tracing how we see things, always start from the object!
- Light travels from the **light source** to the **object**.
- Then it bounces off the object and travels into our **eyes**. Never draw the arrows starting from the eye!
trigger_keywords: path taken by the light rays
